approach: 1. Represent the planets and communication pathways as an undirected graph using an adjacency list. 2. Use a modified Depth-First Search (DFS) or Dijkstra's algorithm where instead of finding the shortest path, you prioritize the highest bandwidth edge at each step. 3. Keep track of the maximum bandwidth achievable on the path to the destination. 4. Return the maximum bandwidth once the destination is reached or indicate that no path exists.
interview_tips: Clarify the constraints of the graph, such as whether it is always connected or if there can be multiple edges between the same nodes. Discuss your thought process as you outline your approach and consider edge cases, such as disconnected graphs or nodes with no outgoing edges.
time_complexity: O(E log V) where E is the number of edges and V is the number of vertices.
space_complexity: O(V) for storing the graph and additional space for maintaining visited nodes and the maximum bandwidth.

title: Treasure Hunt in a 2D Grid with Obstacles
description: You are a treasure hunter in a 2D grid where each cell can be either free (represented by '0') or an obstacle (represented by '1'). You start at the top-left corner of the grid (0,0) and need to find the shortest path to the treasure located at a specific cell (x,y) in the grid. You can move up, down, left, or right, but cannot move through obstacles. If there is no valid path to the treasure, return -1. Your task is to implement a function that takes the grid and the target location of the treasure and returns the length of the shortest path or -1 if unreachable. The grid will be at most 100x100 in size.
approach: 1. Use Breadth-First Search (BFS) to explore the grid, as it efficiently finds the shortest path in an unweighted grid. 2. Initialize a queue with the starting position (0,0) and a variable to track the number of steps taken. 3. Create a set to track visited cells to prevent cycles. 4. For each cell, check its neighboring cells (up, down, left, right). 5. If a neighboring cell is free and not visited, add it to the queue and mark it as visited. 6. If the target cell (x,y) is reached, return the number of steps taken. 7. If the queue is exhausted without reaching the treasure, return -1.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly while outlining your approach. Be sure to discuss the BFS algorithm and its relevance to this problem. Emphasize edge cases, such as starting or ending on an obstacle, and clarify how you'll handle grid boundaries. Practice writing out the BFS loop without running into infinite loops.
time_complexity: O(N * M), where N is the number of rows and M is the number of columns in the grid.
space_complexity: O(N * M) for the visited set and the queue in the BFS.

title: The Mysterious Treasure Hunt Puzzle
description: You are organizing a treasure hunt for a group of friends. Each friend has a unique code that corresponds to a specific location in a park. The codes are made up of integers from 1 to N, where N is the total number of friends. However, thereΓÇÖs a twist: each code can only be used if it has not been used by any other friendΓÇÖs treasure location. Given the total number of friends and a list of codes that each friend has, determine if it's possible to assign each friend a unique treasure location based on their codes. If itΓÇÖs possible, return a list of assigned locations; if not, return an indication that itΓÇÖs not feasible. For instance, if there are 5 friends and the codes are [1, 2, 1, 3, 5], the output should indicate that friend 1 and 3 have overlapping codes and cannot have the same location.
approach: 1. Initialize a set to track used codes. 2. Loop through each friend's code. 3. For each code, check if it has already been used. 4. If it has not been used, add it to the set and record the location. 5. If it has been used, return that itΓÇÖs not feasible. 6. If the loop completes without conflicts, return the list of assigned locations.
interview_tips: Clarify the problem before diving into coding. Discuss edge cases, such as when all friends have the same code or when there are more friends than available unique codes. Explain your thought process as you implement your solution.

title: Treasure Hunt with Distinct Clue Strings
description: You are organizing a treasure hunt for your friends in a park. Each clue consists of a distinct string of characters, and the clues are hidden in various locations. Each clue must lead to the next clue, and your task is to determine if a specific clue can be found further along the path after a sequence of hints. Given an array of distinct clue strings and a target clue string, write a function to check if the target clue is present in the clues array using an efficient searching strategy. The clues can be rearranged in any order after being presented but should be distinct and are only revealed to you in a specific random order. The challenge is to implement your solution with a focus on both time and space efficiency.
approach: 1. Use a hash set or dictionary to store the distinct clue strings for O(1) average case lookup. 2. Traverse through the provided clues array and insert each clue into the hash set. 3. After building the hash set, check if the target clue exists in the set. 4. Return true if it exists, else return false. This approach ensures that you donΓÇÖt have to perform a linear search through the array multiple times.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly as you implement the solution. Discuss the trade-offs of using different data structures for searching and how you ensure uniqueness of clues. Test your function with edge cases, such as an empty array or the target clue being the first/last clue in the list.

title: Lost and Found: Finding the Most Common Lost Item
description: You are given a log of lost items from a large event, where each entry records an item and a timestamp indicating when it was reported lost. Your task is to write a function that determines which item was reported lost the most frequently. In the event of a tie, return the item that was reported lost first based on the timestamp. The log is represented as a list of tuples, where each tuple consists of an item (string) and its timestamp (integer). For example, the input can be like [('watch', 1), ('hat', 2), ('watch', 3), ('hat', 2), ('glasses', 4)]. Your function should return the item that is lost the most frequently along with the count of how many times it was lost. If you find multiple items with the same highest frequency, return the earliest one in terms of its first occurrence in the list.
approach: 1. Create a dictionary to count occurrences of each item. 2. Create another dictionary to store the first occurrence timestamp of each item. 3. Iterate through the logs, updating the count and first occurrence timestamp. 4. After processing all items, find the item with the maximum count, and if there are ties, check the first occurrence timestamps. 5. Return the most frequently lost item and its count.
interview_tips: Talk through your thought process as you build your solution. Focus on edge cases, like empty logs or all items being unique. Make sure to clarify the return requirements in case of ties.
space_complexity: O(U) where U is the number of unique items

title: The Recursive Garden Planner
description: Imagine you are tasked with designing a garden that can accommodate a specific number of plants, arranged in various shapes based on given constraints. The garden's layout can be represented as a 2D grid, where each cell can either contain a plant or be empty. Your goal is to determine all possible unique arrangements of plants that meet the specified conditions: 1) Each plant must be surrounded by an empty cell on all four sides, and 2) No two plants can be adjacent (horizontally or vertically). Write a recursive function that returns the total number of unique arrangements of plants in the garden grid.
approach: 1. Define a base case for the recursion, such as when you've filled the grid or hit the bounds. 2. Use a recursive function that iterates over the grid to try placing a plant in each cell. 3. For each placement, check if the conditions are met (i.e., if the neighboring cells are empty). 4. If valid, mark the cell as occupied and recursively call the function on the next row/column. 5. After returning from the recursive call, backtrack by unmarking the cell to explore other arrangements. 6. Maintain a counter to keep track of valid arrangements found during the recursion.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly as you define the recursive logic. Break down the problem into smaller parts and ensure you handle the base cases well. Discuss potential optimizations, like memoization, to avoid recalculating the same arrangements. Be ready to write test cases to validate your solution.

title: The Recursive Garden Maze Solver
description: You are given a representation of a garden maze as a 2D grid of cells, where '0' represents an open path and '1' represents a wall. The maze has a single entry point at the top-left corner (0,0) and a single exit point at the bottom-right corner (n-1,m-1). Your task is to write a recursive function to determine if there is a valid path from the entry point to the exit point. You must also return the path taken as a list of coordinates in the format [(x1, y1), (x2, y2), ...]. The recursion should explore all valid moves (up, down, left, right) and backtrack when a dead-end is encountered.
approach: 1. Start the recursion from the entry point (0,0). 2. Check if the current cell is out of bounds or a wall ('1'); if so, return false. 3. If the current cell is the exit point (n-1,m-1), add it to the path and return true. 4. Otherwise, mark the cell as visited (for instance, change '0' to '2'). 5. Recursively check all four possible directions (up, down, left, right). 6. If a valid path is found in any direction, append the current cell to the path and return true. 7. If no valid paths are found, backtrack by unmarking the current cell and removing it from the path. 8. Return false if no path is found after exploring all directions.
interview_tips: Discuss your thought process clearly and outline your approach before jumping into coding. Consider edge cases like when the maze is a single cell or when the entry and exit points are the same. Make sure to communicate the base case of your recursion and how you handle visited cells to avoid infinite loops.

title: The Secret Code Challenge
description: You are part of a secret organization that communicates using a unique coding system. Each letter of the alphabet corresponds to a number from 1 to 26 (A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26). You receive a series of encoded messages that are a mix of numbers and letters, where each number represents the letter it corresponds to. However, numbers can sometimes be combined to form two-digit numbers (e.g., 12 can be 'L' and 23 can be 'W'). Your goal is to determine all possible ways to decode a given encoded message consisting of a string of numbers and letters. For example, the string '123' could be decoded as 'ABC', 'LC', or 'AW'. You will need to return all possible decodings in sorted order.
approach: 1. Initialize a list to hold possible decodings. 2. Use backtracking to explore all possible combinations of the encoded string. 3. For each segment of the string, decode it if it's a valid single digit (1-9) or a valid double digit (10-26). 4. When a valid decoding is formed, add it to the list. 5. Sort the list before returning it.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify how the encoding works and ask if there are any constraints on the input (e.g., leading zeros). Also, explain your thought process clearly while implementing the backtracking solution.

title: Magical Transmission: Finding the Bit Pattern for Interstellar Communication
description: In a futuristic scenario, you are a software engineer working for a space exploration company. Your team has discovered a new form of interstellar communication that relies on the manipulation of binary data packets. Each packet can be represented as a non-negative integer. Your task is to find the unique bit pattern that represents the number of packets that can be transmitted without any overlap. Given an integer 'n' representing the total number of binary packets, return the count of unique packets that can be formed using only the bits set to '1' in 'n'. The unique packets must be represented as integers where each integer corresponds to a unique subset of the bits set in 'n'. For example, if n = 5 (binary: 101), the unique packets that can be formed are 1 (001), 4 (100), and 5 (101).
approach: 1. Initialize a counter to zero. 2. Use a loop to iterate through each bit of the integer 'n'. 3. For each bit set to '1', calculate the unique packet by shifting the bit to the left. 4. Increment the counter for each unique packet formed. 5. Return the final count of unique packets.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your thought process and approach before coding. Discuss how you will handle edge cases, such as when 'n' is zero or a power of two. Consider the implications of large integers and bit operations in your explanation.

title: The Mysterious Seating Arrangement
description: In a reality TV show, 10 contestants are seated around a circular table. Each contestant has a unique identifier from 1 to 10. The show host announces a series of mysterious seating rules: 1) Contestant 'A' cannot sit next to contestant 'B', 2) Contestant 'C' must sit directly opposite contestant 'D', 3) Contestants 'E' and 'F' must sit adjacent to each other but not next to 'G'. Given the contestants and the rules, determine a valid seating arrangement. If multiple arrangements are possible, return any one of them. If no arrangement satisfies all the rules, return an indication of failure. You should implement a function that returns the seating arrangement as a list of identifiers.
approach: 1. Start with a circular array representation for the seating arrangement. 2. Use backtracking to explore each possible seating position for the contestants. 3. For each contestant, check the seating rules against the current arrangement to ensure they are satisfied. 4. If a valid configuration is found, return it; otherwise, backtrack and try a different configuration. 5. Keep track of used contestants to avoid repetitions.
interview_tips: Clarify the constraints and ensure you understand the seating arrangement rules. Think aloud to share your thought process and demonstrate your reasoning as you work through potential arrangements.

title: Gardener's Path: Optimizing Flower Planting in a Grid
description: You are given a 2D grid representing a garden. Each cell in the grid can either be empty (denoted by 0) or contain a flower (denoted by 1). You need to find the maximum number of flowers you can plant in the garden such that no two flowers are adjacent (i.e., they cannot be in the same row, same column, or diagonally adjacent). Additionally, you must ensure that the flowers form a connected component, meaning you can walk from any flower to another flower without leaving the grid or crossing empty cells. Write a function that takes the garden grid as input and returns the maximum number of flowers that can be planted following these rules.
approach: 1. Use Depth-First Search (DFS) or Breadth-First Search (BFS) to explore the grid. 2. For each empty cell, simulate planting a flower and check if it remains connected and not adjacent to another flower. 3. Keep track of the maximum number of flowers planted while adhering to the constraints. 4. Consider using dynamic programming to store results of previously calculated states to avoid redundant calculations. 5. Return the maximum count of flowers that can be planted.
interview_tips: Clarify the grid's boundaries and the definition of adjacency with the interviewer. Break down the problem by discussing how you will manage the connected components and adjacency constraints. Think aloud to demonstrate your thought process, and don't hesitate to ask clarifying questions if the problem is complex.
time_complexity: O(N * M * (N + M)) where N is the number of rows and M is the number of columns. This accounts for exploring each cell and its potential neighbors.
space_complexity: O(N * M) for the visited array and recursion stack in DFS.

title: Finding the Longest Path with Leaf Nodes in a Family Tree
description: You are given a binary tree representing a family tree where each node contains a family member's name and their relationship with their parents. The root node represents the grandparent, and each leaf node represents a child with no further descendants. Your task is to find the longest path from the grandparent (root) to any leaf node, and return this path as a list of family member names, along with the length of this path. If there are multiple longest paths, return any one of them.
approach: 1. Perform a depth-first search (DFS) starting from the root node. 2. Keep track of the current path as you traverse down to each leaf. 3. Whenever you reach a leaf node, compare the current path's length with the maximum length found so far. 4. If it exceeds, update the maximum length and store the current path. 5. Finally, return the longest path and its length.
interview_tips: Think recursively and remember to manage the state of the path correctly. Discuss your thought process as you navigate through the tree structure. It's okay to ask for clarifications if the family tree structure has any specific rules or constraints.
time_complexity: O(N), where N is the number of nodes in the tree.
space_complexity: O(H), where H is the height of the tree due to the recursion stack.

title: Lost in the Library: Book Search with Limited Time
description: Imagine you are in a large library that has a unique layout. The books are organized by genre, and each section has a specific number of rows. You have been given a list of books to find, each with a specific shelf location denoted by the genre and row number. Unfortunately, the library has a strict time limit; you can only search through a certain number of rows (let's say K) in each genre before you have to move to the next genre. Your task is to create a function that determines whether you can find all the requested books within the given constraints. The function should return true if you can find all books within K searches in their respective genres, or false otherwise.
approach: 1. Parse the input to separate the list of books into their respective genres and rows. 2. For each genre, count the number of unique rows requested. 3. Compare the count of unique rows with K. If the count exceeds K for any genre, return false. 4. If all genres are within the limit, return true.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your thought process as you break down the problem. Discuss any edge cases, such as when K is very large or very small. Be sure to optimize your solution if possible, and handle input validation.
time_complexity: O(N), where N is the number of books in the input list.
space_complexity: O(G), where G is the number of genres with books you are searching for.

title: The Infinite Gallery Puzzle
description: You are the curator of an infinite art gallery that consists of rooms connected in a circular fashion. Each room can hold a specific number of paintings. You can only move to the next room after placing a certain number of paintings. The twist is that each painting has a different value, and you want to maximize the total value of paintings on display. Given an array of integers representing the values of the paintings, and a limit on how many you can place in each room, determine the maximum value that can be displayed in the gallery by strategically managing the placement across the rooms, considering that you can only move to the next room after placing the specified number of paintings. The placement rules must be adhered to strictly, and you cannot skip any rooms.
approach:
1. Start by defining the constraints of the problem including the number of paintings you can place per room and the total number of rooms. 
2. Use a greedy approach to select the paintings with the highest values to maximize the displayed value. 
3. Implement a loop that iterates through the paintings, placing them according to the room's limit and checking if you can move to the next room. 
4. Keep track of the total value placed and adjust the count of paintings remaining. 
5. Return the total maximum value displayed after processing all paintings.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly, and ensure to discuss edge cases such as when the number of paintings is fewer than the room limit or when all painting values are negative. ItΓÇÖs also helpful to discuss the assumptions youΓÇÖre making about the input.

title: Mystic Tower: The Path of Recursion
description: You are a wizard trapped in a multi-level tower filled with magical creatures. Each level contains a unique set of creatures, and you can only defeat them by casting spells in a particular order. You can only move to the next level if you defeat all the creatures in the current level. Create a recursive function that takes a list of lists, where each inner list represents the creatures on that level. The function should return the total number of ways to defeat all creatures across all levels, considering the order of defeats on each level matters.
approach: To solve the problem, use recursion to calculate the number of permutations of defeating creatures on each level. For each level, the recursive function should consider each creature, calculate the number of ways to defeat the remaining creatures, and sum them up. Base case will be when there are no creatures left in the level, returning 1. Ensure to manage the state between recursive calls.
interview_tips: Clearly articulate your thought process while breaking down the problem. Begin with simple test cases and discuss edge cases, such as empty levels. Consider discussing the base cases and how to combine results from recursive calls. Be prepared to optimize your solution if a brute force approach seems inefficient.
time_complexity: O(n!) where n is the number of creatures in the level (factorial time due to permutations).

title: Treasure Hunt: Finding the Shortest Path in a Maze of Clues
description: You are participating in a treasure hunt where clues are hidden in a rectangular grid representing a maze with obstacles. Each cell in the grid can either be a clue (represented as 'C'), an obstacle (represented as 'X'), or an open path (represented as '.'). Your task is to find the shortest path to collect all clues starting from a designated starting point (denoted by 'S'). You can only move up, down, left, or right, and cannot pass through obstacles. If itΓÇÖs impossible to collect all clues, return -1. Implement a function that takes the grid and starting coordinates as input and returns the length of the shortest path to collect all clues.
approach: 1. Parse the grid to identify the starting point and positions of all clues. 2. Use a breadth-first search (BFS) to explore all possible paths from the starting point, keeping track of the collected clues and the number of steps taken. 3. Maintain a visited set to avoid cycles and unnecessary paths. 4. Continue the BFS until all clues are collected or all possible paths are explored. 5. If all clues are collected, return the minimum steps; otherwise, return -1.
interview_tips: Discuss your thought process clearly while explaining how you will handle edge cases, such as when no clues are present or when obstacles completely block access to clues. Consider the trade-offs of different searching algorithms and explain why you chose BFS for this problem.
time_complexity: O(N * M * 2^K) where N is the number of rows, M is the number of columns, and K is the number of clues.
space_complexity: O(N * M + K) for the visited set and queue during BFS.

title: Social Media Engagement Tracker
description: You are tasked with creating a simplified engagement tracker for a social media platform. Each post on the platform has a unique ID and can be liked by multiple users. Your job is to implement a function that, given a list of user IDs who liked each post, returns a HashMap where the key is the post ID and the value is the count of unique users who liked that post. Additionally, if a user likes a post more than once, it should only be counted once. If a post has no likes, it should not appear in the HashMap.
approach: 1. Initialize an empty HashMap to store post IDs and their unique like counts. 2. Iterate through the list of posts and their respective user IDs. 3. For each post, create a HashSet to store unique user IDs that liked the post. 4. Update the HashMap with the post ID as the key and the size of the HashSet as the value. 5. Return the HashMap containing only posts that have likes.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your thought process and approach as you implement the solution. Discuss edge cases, such as no likes or repeated likes from the same user. Be prepared to analyze the time and space complexity of your solution.
space_complexity: O(U + P) where U is the number of unique users, and P is the number of posts.

title: Sorting Books by Genre and Rating with a Twist
description: You are tasked with organizing a library's collection of books. Each book has a genre and a rating (from 1 to 5 stars). However, the twist is that books of the same genre should be sorted by their ratings in descending order, and if two books have the same rating, they should be organized in alphabetical order by title. Write a function that takes a list of books, where each book is represented as a tuple (title, genre, rating), and returns a sorted list of books according to the specified rules.
approach: 1. Create a custom sorting function that takes into account both genre and rating. 2. First, sort the books by genre (using the genre as the primary key). 3. Then, sort the books by rating in descending order (using the rating as the secondary key). 4. Finally, sort the books with the same genre and rating by title in ascending order. 5. Use the sorted function with a custom key to achieve this multi-level sorting.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly, especially how you handle multiple sorting criteria. Be sure to discuss time complexity and any edge cases, such as books with the same genre and rating. It's also important to mention the data structure you use to store the books.

title: The Reacting Molecule Puzzle
description: You are given a directed graph where each node represents a chemical molecule. Each edge represents a reaction that transforms one molecule into another. Additionally, each reaction has a variable time it takes to complete, represented as weights on the edges. Your task is to find the shortest time required to transform a starting molecule (source node) into a target molecule (target node) while ensuring that each molecule can only be used once in the sequence of reactions. If the target molecule is unreachable from the source, return -1.
approach: 1. Use Dijkstra's algorithm since we are dealing with weighted edges to find the shortest path. 2. Initialize a priority queue to keep track of the current molecule and the time taken to reach it. 3. For each node, explore its neighbors and calculate the cumulative time taken to reach them. 4. Update the neighbor's time if the new calculated time is less than the previously recorded time. 5. Continue until you either find the target molecule or exhaust all possibilities.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify the input format and whether there are any constraints on the number of reactions or molecules. Discuss edge cases, such as scenarios where the source is the same as the target or when there is no valid path to the target. Demonstrating an understanding of graph traversal algorithms and their applications is essential.

title: The Time Traveler's Dilemma: Arranging Events by Past Influence
description: You are a time traveler who can visit different historical events, but you have a peculiar constraint: you can only attend events that are influenced by previous ones in a specific manner. Given a list of events where each event has a time and a list of events that influence it, your task is to determine the order in which you should experience these events. Each event will have a unique time and a set of events that must have occurred before it. Return the valid sequence of events in chronological order that respects these influences, or indicate that it is impossible to attend them all due to circular dependencies.
approach: To solve this problem, we can use a directed graph representation where events are nodes and directed edges represent the influence relationships. We can perform a topological sort on this directed graph. If the sort completes successfully, it indicates a valid order of events. If we detect a cycle during the sorting process, it means that not all events can be attended due to circular dependencies.
interview_tips: Start by clarifying the problem with the interviewer, especially regarding how influences are defined. Discuss your thought process openly, especially your choice of data structures. Remember to handle edge cases, such as cycles in the dependency graph. It may be helpful to draw diagrams to visualize the relationships.

title: The Three-Way Traffic Light Dilemma
description: Imagine a city with three intersecting roads, each governed by a traffic light. Each light can be either RED, YELLOW, or GREEN. A safe cross occurs when all lights are not RED at the same time. However, if any light is YELLOW, it indicates that the light may soon change to RED, meaning cars on that road should not be moving. Write a function that takes the status of the three traffic lights (each represented as a string) and determines if it's safe for pedestrians to cross. A 'YES' output means it's safe, and 'NO' means it's not. Your function should also consider edge cases where any light stays RED indefinitely.
approach: 1. Read the status of the three traffic lights. 2. Create a set of conditions that identify when it is safe to cross:  - At least one light should be GREEN, or  - If it is YELLOW, the others must not be RED. 3. Implement checks based on these conditions and return 'YES' or 'NO'.
interview_tips: Clarify any assumptions before coding. Discuss the traffic light behaviors and edge cases openly with the interviewer. Think of all possible combinations and how to handle them logically.

title: Generate Palindrome Subsets from the Input String
description: Given an input string, your task is to determine all possible subsets that can form palindromic strings. For example, if the input string is 'aabb', the palindromic subsets would include 'a', 'aa', 'b', 'bb', 'abba', and 'aab'. Your solution should return a list of unique palindromic subsets without duplicates, sorted in lexicographical order. Note that a single character is also considered a valid palindrome.
approach: 1. Use a backtracking algorithm to generate all possible subsets of the input string. 2. For each generated subset, check if it is a palindrome. 3. If it is a palindrome, add it to a set to avoid duplicates. 4. Once all subsets have been checked, convert the set to a list and sort it lexicographically before returning the result.
interview_tips: Focus on the backtracking technique to generate subsets efficiently. Be prepared to explain the palindrome checking mechanism and ensure you handle edge cases, such as empty strings or strings with only one character. Demonstrating your ability to optimize the solution will be beneficial.

title: Palindrome Substring Finder with Character Frequency Constraint
description: Given a string s and an integer k, write a function that identifies all unique substrings of s that are palindromes and contain at most k unique characters. For instance, for the string 'aabbcc' and k = 2, the valid palindromic substrings would be 'aa', 'bb', 'cc', 'aabaa', 'bb', and 'bccb' would not be included due to having more than 2 unique characters. The output should be a list of these palindromic substrings sorted by their first appearance in the original string.
approach: 1. Initialize an empty set to hold unique palindromic substrings. 2. Iterate through the string using two nested loops to generate substrings. 3. For each substring, check if it is a palindrome. 4. If it is, verify if the number of unique characters in it is less than or equal to k. 5. If both conditions are satisfied, add the substring to the set. 6. Finally, return the sorted list of unique palindromic substrings.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your thought process while discussing edge cases, such as empty strings or strings with all identical characters. Make sure to validate the inputs and handle various scenarios efficiently. It's also good to discuss time complexity and how you can optimize the palindrome check.

title: Bitwise Segment Flipper
description: You are given an array of integers where each integer can be represented as a 32-bit binary number. Your task is to determine the maximum number of contiguous segments in the array that can be flipped by exactly one operation. A 'flip' operation on a segment means inverting all bits (0s to 1s and 1s to 0s) of the integers in that segment. A segment is defined by two indices (i, j) such that 0 Γëñ i Γëñ j < n. The challenge is to find the maximum number of segments that can be flipped while ensuring that all integers in those segments have the same parity of their sum after the flip. Return the maximum count of such segments. For example, given the input array [1, 2, 3], the binary representations are [01, 10, 11]. Flipping the segment [2, 3] would yield [01, 01], where the sum remains even.
approach: 1. Start by calculating the binary representations of each integer in the array. 2. Iterate through the array to maintain a count of segments that can be formed with a parity check on their sums. 3. For each potential segment defined by indices (i, j), simulate the flip and check if the new sum of the segment meets the parity condition. 4. Keep track of the maximum number of valid segments encountered during the iterations.
interview_tips: Focus on understanding how bitwise operations affect the numbers. Discuss your thought process clearly. Consider edge cases, especially with arrays containing zeros and negative numbers. Explain your solution in steps, and don't hesitate to clarify the problem requirements if needed.

title: Customer Purchase Analysis with Window Functions
description: You have a table named 'Purchases' that contains the following columns: 'CustomerID', 'PurchaseDate', and 'Amount'. Your task is to calculate the total amount spent by each customer in the last three purchases. Additionally, return the 'CustomerID' and the 'TotalSpent' for customers who have made at least three purchases. The output should be ordered by 'TotalSpent' in descending order.
approach: 1. Use window functions to rank purchases for each customer ordered by the purchase date. 2. Filter to keep only the last three purchases. 3. Group the results by 'CustomerID' and sum the 'Amount' for each customer. 4. Finally, filter out customers with less than three purchases and order the results by 'TotalSpent' in descending order.
interview_tips: Make sure to clearly explain the purpose of each SQL clause you use. Practice window functions and understand how partitioning and ordering work. Discuss potential edge cases, such as customers with fewer than three purchases, and how you'd handle them.

title: Mystery Box Distribution
description: You are tasked with distributing 'mystery boxes' among 'n' friends. Each box can contain a random number of items. The goal is to ensure that every friend ends up with a different number of items. However, you can only add or remove items from the boxes, and you cannot split the items between boxes. Given an array of integers representing the current number of items in each box, determine the minimum number of items you need to add or remove to achieve the distribution criteria. If it is impossible, return -1. For example, for the input [1, 2, 1], the output should be 2 (remove 1 from the first box and add 1 to the second box making it [1, 2, 3]).
approach: 1. Start by sorting the array of items in each box. 2. Traverse through the sorted list and keep track of the desired number of items for each box starting from 1. 3. If the current box has items less than the desired number, calculate the difference and add it to a counter. 4. If the current box has items greater than or equal to the desired number, simply move to the next box. If two boxes have the same number of items, also calculate the adjustments necessary to make them unique. 5. Return the total count of adjustments made. If at any point, the adjustments needed are negative or impossible to make unique, return -1.
interview_tips: Be sure to clarify the problem constraints and make sure you understand how many boxes and their current contents can be adjusted. Think out loud as you work through the logic of your approach. Edge cases such as empty boxes or only one friend should also be discussed.

title: Tree of Distinct Sums: Finding Unique Paths Based on Sum Constraints
description:
You are given a binary tree where each node contains an integer value. Your task is to find all unique paths from the root to any leaf node such that the sum of the values along the path is greater than a given threshold K. A path is considered unique if it has a distinct sum, regardless of the nodes visited. Return the count of such unique paths that satisfy the sum condition. For example, consider the tree: 

        10
       /  \
      5    15
     / \     \
    3   7    20

If K = 15, the valid paths are: [10, 5, 3] (sum = 18), [10, 5, 7] (sum = 22), and [10, 15, 20] (sum = 45). Thus, the output should be 3.
approach:
1. Perform a depth-first search (DFS) starting from the root node. 
2. Maintain a running sum of the values along the current path. 
3. When reaching a leaf node, check if the current path's sum exceeds K. 
4. If it does, add this sum to a set to ensure uniqueness. 
5. Continue this process until all paths are explored, then return the size of the set which contains the unique sums.
interview_tips: Clarify the definition of unique paths and ensure you understand the tree structure. Discuss your thought process while implementing DFS and how you will handle the sum tracking. Don't forget to consider edge cases such as an empty tree or trees with only one node.
time_complexity: O(N), where N is the number of nodes in the tree, as each node is visited once.
space_complexity: O(H), where H is the height of the tree due to recursive stack space, and in the worst case (for a skewed tree), it could be O(N).

title: Graph of Time Travelers
description: Imagine a world where time travelers can visit different points in history, represented as nodes in a directed graph. Each node represents a distinct historical event. There are directed edges between these nodes that indicate the ability for a time traveler to visit one event after another, with weights representing the time cost (in years) to travel between them. Your task is to determine the minimum total time cost for a time traveler to start from a given event and return to it after visiting a specified number of unique events. If it is impossible to visit the required number of events due to the constraints of the graph, return -1.
approach: 1. Use a Depth-First Search (DFS) or Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm to explore all possible paths from the starting node. 2. Maintain a count of unique events visited. 3. Keep track of the total time cost incurred for each path. 4. If the required number of unique events has been visited, check if a path leads back to the starting event. 5. Use memoization to avoid recalculating paths that have already been explored. 6. Return the minimum time cost found or -1 if no valid path exists.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your thought process as you describe the graph traversal method you choose. Discuss why you might select DFS or BFS based on the specific requirements of the problem. Be ready to handle edge cases, such as cycles in the graph and unreachable nodes. Think aloud about your approach to optimizing search paths to improve efficiency.
time_complexity: O(N^2 * E) where N is the number of nodes and E is the number of edges, depending on the traversal method and the memoization strategy used.
space_complexity: O(N) for the recursion stack and memoization storage.

title: The Array Juggler: Balancing Elements to Form a Circle
description: You are given an array of integers representing the weights of different balls. Your task is to determine if it's possible to rearrange these balls in a circular fashion such that the absolute difference between the weights of any two adjacent balls in the circle does not exceed a given threshold 'k'. If such an arrangement is possible, return 'Yes'; otherwise, return 'No'. Note that you may not use any ball more than once in the arrangement.
approach: 1. First, sort the array of weights. 2. Check the absolute difference between the maximum weight and the minimum weight in the sorted array. 3. If this difference is greater than 'k', return 'No'. 4. Next, check the difference between the last and first element in the sorted array since they will be adjacent in a circular arrangement. 5. If all differences are within 'k', return 'Yes'.
interview_tips: Start by discussing your thought process and how you would approach the problem. Consider edge cases, such as arrays with less than 3 elements or all elements being the same. Be ready to explain your sorting choice and how it helps simplify the problem.

title: Sort the Gift Packages by Priority and Weight
description: You are tasked with sorting gift packages for a charity event. Each package has a priority level (an integer) and a weight (a positive integer). The goal is to sort the packages first by priority in descending order; if two packages have the same priority, they should be sorted by weight in ascending order. Implement a function to perform this sorting operation. For example, given the following list of packages: [(3, 5), (1, 2), (3, 8), (2, 1)], the sorted output should be [(3, 5), (3, 8), (2, 1), (1, 2)].
approach: To solve this problem, we will use a sorting algorithm that allows for custom comparison. We can leverage Python's in-built sort functionality with a custom key. The key will be a tuple where the first element is the negative of the priority (to achieve descending order) and the second element is the weight (for ascending order). This way, the sort function will first consider priority and then weight in case of ties.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your sorting strategy before coding. Discuss the properties of the data that are important, such as how to handle ties in priority. Make sure to test with edge cases such as all packages having the same priority or weight.

title: Tree Traversal with Color Coding
description: You are given a binary tree where each node is associated with a color (either 'red' or 'blue'). Your task is to implement a function that performs a depth-first traversal of the tree and returns two lists: one containing the values of all the 'red' nodes encountered during the traversal, and another containing the values of all the 'blue' nodes. The function should take the root of the binary tree as input and return the two lists. Ensure that the nodes are processed in pre-order (root, left, right) fashion.
approach: 1. Define a helper function that takes the current node and two lists as parameters. 2. If the current node is null, return. 3. Check the color of the current node; if it is 'red', append its value to the red list; if 'blue', append it to the blue list. 4. Recursively call the helper function for the left child and then for the right child. 5. In the main function, initialize the two lists and call the helper function with the root node. 6. Return the two lists.
interview_tips: Focus on the traversal method and ensure you handle null nodes appropriately. Discuss your approach before coding, and consider edge cases such as empty trees or trees with only one color. Optimize your recursive function to avoid unnecessary processing.
space_complexity: O(H) where H is the height of the tree (for the recursion stack)

title: Finding Top 3 Most Engaged Users in a Social Media Platform
description: You are working with a database for a social media platform that records user interactions. The platform has a table `UserInteractions` with the following schema: `UserId INT`, `InteractionType VARCHAR`, `InteractionDate DATETIME`. The `InteractionType` can be 'like', 'comment', or 'share'. Your task is to write a SQL query that retrieves the top 3 users with the highest total number of interactions (likes, comments, and shares) over the last 30 days. The output should include `UserId` and the total interaction count, ordered by the count in descending order. If there are fewer than 3 users, just return those available.
approach: 1. Filter the records from the `UserInteractions` table where the `InteractionDate` is within the last 30 days. 2. Group the results by `UserId` and use the COUNT function to get the total number of interactions for each user. 3. Order the results by the total interaction count in descending order. 4. Use the LIMIT clause to return only the top 3 users. 5. Handle cases where fewer than 3 users exist by simply returning all found users.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify any assumptions, like how dates will be handled and if the InteractionTypes are fixed. Discuss indexing on the `InteractionDate` if performance becomes a concern and mention how you would handle ties in interaction counts.

title: Optimal Path in a Weighted City Grid with Dynamic Weather Changes
description: You are given a grid representing a city where each cell contains a weight that represents the cost to move through that cell. Additionally, the weather changes dynamically, meaning that every hour, certain paths may become more or less expensive depending on weather conditions (like rain or snow). You must optimize your route to get from the top-left corner of the grid to the bottom-right corner, while accounting for potential changes in weights over a specified number of hours. The weather changes are provided as a list of events with their associated impacts on the grid weights. Your goal is to find the minimum cost of your journey considering the dynamic changes.
approach: 1. Represent the grid and initialize a cost matrix to keep track of minimum costs to reach each cell. 2. Use dynamic programming to compute the minimum cost path through the grid, considering only the initial weights. 3. For each weather change event, update the weights on the grid accordingly. 4. Recalculate the minimum cost from the top-left to the bottom-right, taking the updated weights into account. 5. Use a priority queue (min-heap) to efficiently retrieve the next cell to process based on the lowest cumulative cost.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your thought process as you approach the problem. Make sure to discuss how you would handle edge cases, such as no valid path due to excessive weights or changes. Be prepared to discuss the trade-offs of implementing the solution using different data structures.

title: Anagram Grouping in a Dynamic Document Manager
description: You are developing a document management system that needs to categorize documents based on their content. Each document can be represented as a string. Your task is to implement a function that takes a list of document strings and groups them into collections of anagrams. The output should be a list of lists, where each inner list contains strings that are anagrams of each other. For example, given the input ['bat', 'tab', 'cat'], the output should be [['bat', 'tab'], ['cat']]. In addition, consider the following constraints: Documents can have varying lengths, and the input may contain duplicates. You should ensure that your method handles these cases efficiently.
approach: 1. Create a HashMap to map the sorted version of each document string to its corresponding list of anagrams. 2. For each document, sort the characters in the string to obtain a unique key. 3. Check if this key exists in the HashMap; if it does, append the document to the list corresponding to that key. If it doesn't exist, create a new entry with the document as the first element in the list. 4. Finally, extract the values (the lists of anagrams) from the HashMap and return them as the result.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your thought process before jumping into coding. Discuss edge cases, such as handling duplicate documents and how your approach can scale with a large number of inputs. Be prepared to analyze the time and space complexity of your solution and discuss potential optimizations.
time_complexity: O(N * K log K), where N is the number of documents and K is the maximum length of a document string due to sorting.
space_complexity: O(N * K), where N is the number of documents and K is the maximum length of a document string, to store the lists of anagrams.

title: Array of Uniques: The Frequency Jigsaw
description: You have been tasked with creating a puzzle game where players need to identify a sequence of unique numbers based on their frequencies. Given an array of integers, your goal is to return a new array that contains only the integers that appear exactly once in the original array, sorted in ascending order. For example, given the input [4, 3, 2, 7, 8, 3, 2, 1], the output should be [1, 4, 7, 8]. Your function should handle both positive and negative integers, and you must ensure the solution is efficient.
approach: 1. Initialize a frequency map (using a dictionary or hash table) to count occurrences of each element in the array. 2. Iterate through the original array and populate the frequency map. 3. Create a new array to store the elements that occur exactly once by filtering the frequency map. 4. Sort the resultant array in ascending order. 5. Return the sorted array as the final output.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify the requirements with the interviewer, particularly about the input constraints and whether the array can include negative numbers. Discuss your approach before coding and consider edge cases such as an empty array or an array where all elements are duplicated.
time_complexity: O(N log N) due to the sorting step, where N is the number of elements in the input array.
space_complexity: O(N) for the frequency map and the result array.

title: Find the Missing Treasure Point
description: In a treasure hunt, you are given a list of coordinates (x, y) of treasure points. Each point can be represented as a tuple (x, y). However, one of the points is missing from the list, and your goal is to identify the missing point based on the property that every other point has a unique x-coordinate and a unique y-coordinate. You can assume that the given points are in the range of 1 to N for both x and y. Write a function that finds this missing point efficiently.
approach: 1. Create two sets to track the existing x and y coordinates from the given list of points. 2. Iterate through the points and populate these sets. 3. Identify the missing x-coordinate by comparing the set with the range from 1 to N. 4. Repeat the same for y-coordinates. 5. The missing point will be (missing_x, missing_y).
interview_tips: Clarify the input constraints and ask if there are any edge cases to consider. Discuss your approach before diving into coding. Remember to handle cases where all points are provided but one is missing efficiently.

title: Office Party Seating Arrangement
description: You are organizing an office party and need to arrange your colleagues around tables in such a way that maximizes the overall enjoyment based on their friendship levels. Each colleague has a friendship score with every other colleague which can be a positive or negative integer. Given a list of these scores in a matrix format where score[i][j] indicates the enjoyment score for the pair (i, j), determine the maximum total enjoyment score achievable by seating exactly K colleagues around a single round table. The arrangement should ensure that each colleague interacts with their two immediate neighbors. Note: It's guaranteed that K will not exceed the total number of colleagues available.
approach: 1. First, generate all possible combinations of K colleagues from the total number of colleagues. 2. For each combination, calculate the total enjoyment score by summing up the scores of each pair of adjacent colleagues, including the wrap-around pair (last and first colleague). 3. Use dynamic programming to memoize results of previously computed subsets to avoid redundant calculations. 4. Return the maximum total enjoyment score found across all combinations.
interview_tips: Discuss your thought process and how you plan to tackle the problem before diving into coding. Make sure to explain how you ensure optimal performance using dynamic programming and memoization. Be prepared to handle edge cases, such as negative scores or small values for K.

title: Optimal Tiling of a Chessboard with Dominoes
description: You have an N x N chessboard where you are allowed to use dominoes of size 1 x 2. A domino can be placed either horizontally or vertically. Your task is to calculate the maximum number of dominoes that can completely fill the chessboard without any leftover squares. Additionally, if the chessboard has a specific number of squares marked as unusable (like obstacles), you need to account for these in your calculation. Return the maximum number of dominoes that can be placed. For simplicity, you can assume that N is always even and the unusable squares are given as a list of coordinates.
approach: 1. Create a 2D array to represent the chessboard and mark the unusable squares. 2. Use a recursive approach with memoization to explore all possible placements of dominoes on the board. 3. At each step, check if a domino can be placed, then move to the next available square recursively. 4. Use dynamic programming to store results of subproblems (placements for subsections of the board). 5. Return the maximum count of domino placements.
interview_tips: Discuss your thought process clearly. Start with base cases and small examples to demonstrate your understanding. If you get stuck, consider simpler versions of the problem and build up. Be prepared to explain your memoization strategy, as itΓÇÖs crucial in dynamic programming.

title: String Composition Analysis
description: You are given a list of strings representing a sequence of words. Your task is to determine if you can form a target string by concatenating a specific number of words from that list, where each word can only be used once. The target string must be formed in the exact order of the words in the list. If it's possible, return the minimum number of words used to form the target string. If not, return -1. For example, given the list ['quick', 'brown', 'fox'] and target 'quickbrown', the output should be 2 since 'quick' + 'brown' forms the target string.
approach: 1. Initialize a counter for words used and a pointer for the position in the target string. 2. Iterate through the list of words, checking if the target string starts with the current word from the list. 3. If it does, increment the counter and move the pointer forward in the target string accordingly. 4. Continue this until you either form the target string completely (return the counter) or exhaust the list without forming it (return -1).
interview_tips: Clarify the requirements and edge cases with the interviewer. Consider examples and ask for input constraints. Discuss your thought process clearly as you iterate through the list of words and target string. It's important to handle cases where words might overlap or when the target string is empty.
time_complexity: O(N * M), where N is the number of words and M is the average length of words.
space_complexity: O(1), as we're only using a few counters and pointers.

title: String Palindrome Traffic Lights
description: Imagine you are designing a traffic light system that must generate and display a string of colors based on incoming traffic. Each light (Red, Yellow, Green) is represented by a letter (R, Y, G). The string must not only represent the light sequence but also must be a palindrome to ensure it mirrors the traffic flow in both directions. Write a function that takes an input string representing traffic lights and checks whether it can be rearranged to form a palindrome. If it can be rearranged, return 'YES'; otherwise, return 'NO'. For example, 'RYYG' can be rearranged to 'GYRYG' but 'RYYR' cannot. Your function should also handle edge cases such as empty strings and invalid characters.
approach: 1. Count the occurrences of each character in the string using a dictionary or similar structure. 2. Check the counts; for a string to be rearranged into a palindrome, at most one character can have an odd count. 3. Based on these counts, return 'YES' if the conditions are met, otherwise return 'NO'.
interview_tips: Clarify the requirements and constraints of the problem before jumping to code. Discuss how you plan to handle edge cases, and think aloud while coding, especially about the logic behind checking for palindrome properties.

title: Treasure Hunt: Optimal Path Searching on a Map
description: You are given a grid representing a treasure map, where each cell contains a positive integer indicating the 'treasure value' of that location. Also, there are several obstacles on this map that cannot be crossed, represented by -1 in the grid. Your task is to find the maximum treasure value that can be collected by moving from the top-left corner (0,0) to the bottom-right corner (n-1,m-1) of the grid. You can only move right or down. If it is not possible to reach the bottom-right corner due to obstacles, return -1. The grid is guaranteed to have at least one cell with a value of 0 or greater (not an obstacle).
approach: 1. Use dynamic programming to maintain a 2D array where each cell represents the maximum treasure value collected up to that point. 2. Initialize the starting point (0,0) with its treasure value if it's not an obstacle. 3. Iterate through the grid, updating each cell's maximum value based on the values above and to the left, if they are not obstacles. 4. Return the value at the bottom-right corner of the DP table, or -1 if it's still uninitialized (which indicates that it's unreachable).
interview_tips: Clarify the movement constraints and how to handle obstacles. Explain your thought process as you lay out the DP table. Make sure to discuss edge cases, such as grids filled with obstacles and the smallest possible grid (1x1).

title: Treasure Map: Finding the Shortest Path with Hidden Traps
description: You are given a 2D grid representing a treasure map where 'T' denotes a treasure, 'X' denotes a trap, and '.' denotes a safe cell. Your task is to find the shortest path from the starting position (0,0) to the treasure while avoiding all traps. You can move up, down, left, or right. If there is no path to the treasure, return -1. The grid dimensions will not exceed 100 x 100.
approach: 1. Use Breadth-First Search (BFS) to explore the grid from the starting point (0,0). 2. Maintain a queue for the current positions to explore and a set or matrix to track visited cells. 3. For each position, check all four possible moves (up, down, left, right). 4. If the next position is a trap ('X'), skip that move. 5. If you reach the treasure ('T'), return the number of steps taken. 6. If the queue is empty and the treasure hasn't been found, return -1.
interview_tips: Clarify the constraints and the representation of the grid. Think aloud while implementing the BFS to show your thought process. Consider edge cases where the grid may be completely filled with traps, or the treasure is unreachable.
time_complexity: O(N * M), where N is the number of rows and M is the number of columns in the grid.
space_complexity: O(N * M) for the queue and visited tracking.

title: Customer Churn Analysis: Identifying High-Risk Accounts
description: You are working for an e-commerce company and need to analyze customer behavior to predict churn. You have a table called `customers` with the following columns: `customer_id`, `signup_date`, `last_purchase_date`, and `total_spent`. Your task is to identify customers who are at high risk of churning. A customer is considered high-risk if they have not made a purchase in the last 6 months and their total spent is less than $100. Write an SQL query that returns the `customer_id` and `total_spent` of these high-risk customers, sorted by `total_spent` in ascending order.
approach:
1. Use the current date to calculate the date 6 months ago. 
2. Filter records in the `customers` table based on `last_purchase_date` being less than this calculated date. 
3. Further filter the results to include only customers with `total_spent` less than $100. 
4. Select the relevant fields (`customer_id`, `total_spent`) and sort the results by `total_spent` in ascending order.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify assumptions, such as the data type of dates. Discuss your thought process as you write the SQL query and explain how you are filtering and sorting the data. Be prepared to explain why you chose specific functions or methods.

title: Finding Stale Inventory Items Based on Last Purchase Date
description: Given a database of products and their purchase history, write an SQL query to identify products that have not been purchased in the last 90 days. The database consists of two tables: 'products' (with columns 'product_id', 'product_name', and 'category') and 'purchases' (with columns 'purchase_id', 'product_id', 'purchase_date'). Your task is to retrieve the product ID and product name of all products that have not had any purchases in the last 90 days, along with a count of how long it has been since each product was last purchased. Sort the results by the count of days since the last purchase in descending order.
approach: 1. Use a LEFT JOIN to connect the 'products' table with the 'purchases' table on 'product_id'. 2. Utilize the COALESCE function to handle products that have never been purchased. 3. Use the CURRENT_DATE function to calculate the difference between today and the latest purchase date. 4. Filter the results where the difference is greater than 90 days. 5. Select the product ID, product name, and the count of days since the last purchase. 6. Sort the final results based on the count of days in descending order.
interview_tips: Ensure you understand the concepts of JOIN operations and date functions in SQL. Practice writing queries to handle NULL values, as they can occur when products have never been purchased. Discuss your thought process clearly while writing the query, and test it with edge cases in mind.

title: Optimal Restaurant Reservation Manager
description: You are tasked with creating a reservation system for a restaurant that can handle multiple bookings at the same time. Each reservation consists of a party size and a unique customer ID. The restaurant can accommodate a maximum number of guests at once. Your goal is to implement a function that takes a list of reservations (each defined by a customer ID and party size) and determines if all reservations can be accommodated at the same time, without exceeding the restaurant's capacity. If any reservation cannot be accommodated, return the customer ID of the reservation that causes the overflow. If all reservations can be accommodated, return 'All reservations successful'.
approach: 1. Create a variable to track the total number of guests currently reserved. 2. Iterate through the list of reservations. For each reservation, check if adding the party size exceeds the restaurant's capacity. 3. If it exceeds, return the customer ID of that reservation. 4. If all reservations can be accommodated, return 'All reservations successful'. Use a HashMap to store customer IDs and their corresponding party sizes, which can help in efficiently checking and managing reservations.
interview_tips: Discuss your thought process clearly as you write your code. Explain how you are utilizing the HashMap to track reservations. Consider edge cases, such as overlapping reservations or maximum capacity. Be prepared to discuss how changes in requirements might affect your approach.

title: Treasure Hunt in a 2D Grid
description: You are participating in a treasure hunt represented by a 2D grid. The grid consists of different types of land: 'T' for treasure, 'O' for open land, and 'X' for obstacles. The objective is to find the shortest path from your starting position (marked by 'S') to the closest treasure 'T', while avoiding obstacles 'X'. There can be multiple treasures in the grid. If no treasure is reachable, return -1. Your function should return the coordinates of the closest treasure found in the form of a tuple (row, column). If multiple treasures are equidistant, return the first one found in reading order (top to bottom, left to right).
approach:
1. Initialize a queue for breadth-first search (BFS) and enqueue the starting position 'S'. 
2. Maintain a set to keep track of visited coordinates to avoid cycles. 
3. For each position, explore its adjacent cells (up, down, left, right). 
4. If an adjacent cell is a treasure 'T', return its coordinates. 
5. If it is an open land 'O' and not visited, enqueue it and mark it as visited. 
6. If the queue is exhausted and no treasure is found, return -1.
interview_tips: Clearly communicate your thought process while explaining your approach. Discuss edge cases such as no reachable treasures or multiple treasures at the same distance. Consider time complexity and optimizations during your explanation.

title: Identify Frequent Customer Purchases with Time-Window Analysis
description: Given a table named 'Purchases' containing columns 'customer_id', 'purchase_date', and 'item_id', write a SQL query to identify customers who have purchased the same item more than once within a 30-day window. The output should include 'customer_id', 'item_id', and the count of purchases within that time frame. If a customer has purchased an item multiple times but not within a 30-day window, they should not be included in the output.
approach: To solve this problem, follow these steps: 1. Use a self-join on the 'Purchases' table to compare purchase dates. 2. Set a condition to check if the purchase dates are within 30 days of each other. 3. Group the results by 'customer_id' and 'item_id' to count occurrences. 4. Use the HAVING clause to filter the results to only include instances where the count exceeds one.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify the time frame and the grouping requirements with your interviewer. Discuss your thought process as you write the query and consider edge cases, such as purchases on the same day or at different times. Testing your SQL query on sample data can help ensure it works as expected.
time_complexity: O(N^2) due to self-join on the same table.
space_complexity: O(N) for storage of intermediate results.

title: The Mysterious Timekeeper
description: In a futuristic world, a timekeeper has the ability to manipulate time but can only perform a limited number of time jumps per day. Each time jump causes a ripple effect on the timeline. You are given a list of events represented by their timestamps (in seconds since midnight) and a number 'k' which indicates how many time jumps the timekeeper can make. Your task is to determine the maximum number of events that can be attended by making at most 'k' time jumps to skip over conflicting events. An event can be attended if its start time does not overlap with any already attended event's end time. Return the maximum number of events that can be attended.
approach: 1. Sort the list of events based on their end times. 2. Use a greedy approach to iterate through the events and maintain a count of attended events. 3. Keep track of the last attended event's end time and the number of time jumps made. 4. If the next event starts after the last attended event's end time, attend it. 5. If it overlaps, decide if a time jump can be made to skip it while keeping track of the number of jumps used. 6. Continue this until all events are considered or the maximum jumps allowed are used.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly while discussing the problem. Write out your plan before coding and discuss edge cases. Make sure to ask clarifying questions about the input format and constraints. Emphasize the importance of handling overlaps and the greedy strategy in your solution.

title: Grocery Store Inventory Management
description: Imagine you're developing an inventory management system for a grocery store. Each time a customer makes a purchase, you need to update the inventory count. However, you also want to track which items went out of stock. Given a list of transactions (each transaction includes the item name and the quantity purchased), write a function that uses a HashMap to manage the inventory and returns a list of items that are out of stock after processing all transactions.
approach: 1. Initialize an empty HashMap to store item names as keys and their quantities as values. 2. Iterate through the list of transactions, updating the HashMap based on the quantity purchased. 3. After processing all transactions, create a list to store items that have a quantity of zero. 4. Return this list of out-of-stock items.
interview_tips: Think about edge cases such as items that were never in stock or transactions where the quantity exceeds what was available. Be ready to discuss how you would handle such scenarios. Also, practice articulating your thought process clearly as you implement the solution.

title: Vowel-Consonant Swap Challenge
description: You are given a string that consists of lowercase alphabetic characters. Your task is to swap all vowels with the next consonant in the string while preserving the order of the characters. If a vowel is followed by another vowel, continue to swap until you reach a consonant. If a vowel appears at the end of the string, it should remain unchanged. For example, given the input 'aeiou', the output should be 'bcdfg', and for 'hello', it should be 'hfnmp'. Write a function to perform this transformation.
approach:
1. Create a set of vowels for quick lookup. 
2. Initialize an output list to build the resulting string. 
3. Iterate through each character in the input string. 
4. If the character is a vowel, find the next consonant and add it to the output list. 
5. If the character is a consonant, just append it to the output list. 
6. Finally, join the output list into a string and return it.
interview_tips: Clarify edge cases, such as handling consecutive vowels and the end of the string. Discuss your thought process while iterating through the characters. Pay attention to efficiency, especially if the string length can be large.

title: Finding the Optimal Subarray for Seasonal Sales
description: You are given an array of integers, where each integer represents sales data for a particular day over a year (365 days). Your task is to find the contiguous subarray that maximizes the total sales during the peak sales season, which is defined as any 90-day interval within the year. Return both the maximum sales value and the starting and ending indices of the subarray. If there are multiple solutions, return the one with the earliest start index.
approach: 1. Initialize variables to keep track of the maximum sales found, the current sum of the sales over the last 90 days, and the starting index of the current subarray. 2. Use a sliding window technique to iterate through the array. For each new day, add the current day's sales to the current sum. 3. If the window exceeds 90 days, subtract the sales from the day that is leaving the window. 4. After updating the current sum, check if it exceeds the maximum sales recorded and update the maximum sales and indices accordingly. 5. Continue until all days have been processed.
interview_tips: Make sure to explain your thought process as you describe the approach. Discuss edge cases, such as when the length of the array is less than 90 days. Also, clarify your choice of the sliding window approach and how it optimizes your solution. Finally, run through a couple of test cases to showcase your solution's correctness.

title: Sorting a Mixed Array of Strings and Numbers by Custom Rule
description: You are given an array consisting of both strings and integers. Your task is to sort this array such that all integers appear before any strings, and among the integers, they should be sorted in ascending order. For strings, they should be sorted in descending order. Implement a function that performs this sort. For example, given the input [3, 'apple', 1, 'banana', 2], the output should be [1, 2, 3, 'banana', 'apple'].
approach: 1. Create two separate lists: one for integers and another for strings. 2. Traverse the original array and append each element to its respective list based on its type. 3. Sort the integers list in ascending order. 4. Sort the strings list in descending order. 5. Concatenate the two sorted lists (integers first, then strings) and return the result.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly before coding. Discuss potential edge cases, such as handling empty arrays or arrays with only one type of element. Make sure to test your solution with different input scenarios after implementing it.

title: The Lighthouse Graph: Navigating Light Signals Between Islands
description: You are given a series of islands connected by bridges, represented as a directed graph. Each island is equipped with a lighthouse that can send light signals to other lighthouses on adjacent islands through the bridges. The signal takes one unit of time to travel to the next island. Given a starting island and a time limit, determine how many distinct islands can receive the light signal within the given time. The goal is to identify all reachable islands within the time limit and return their count. If the starting island is not connected to any other islands, the count should be zero.
approach:
1. Represent the islands and bridges as a directed graph using an adjacency list.
2. Implement a breadth-first search (BFS) or depth-first search (DFS) starting from the given starting island.
3. Keep track of visited islands to avoid counting them multiple times.
4. For each island reached, check if the time taken is within the limit. If so, increase the count of reachable islands.
5. Return the total count of distinct reachable islands after exploring all possible paths within the time limit.
interview_tips: Clearly explain your thought process as you construct the graph and navigate through it. Discuss the choice of BFS or DFS and when one might be preferred over the other. Be prepared to handle edge cases, such as isolated islands or when the starting island is the only one in the graph.
time_complexity: O(V + E) where V is the number of vertices (islands) and E is the number of edges (bridges).
space_complexity: O(V) for the visited array and graph representation.

title: Tree Structure for Dynamic Event Scheduling
description: You are tasked with designing a tree-based structure to manage a dynamic schedule for events in a concert hall. Each event has a start time and an end time. The tree should allow for the following operations: adding an event, removing an event, and querying for all events happening at a specific time. If two events overlap, they cannot be scheduled at the same time. Your goal is to implement the add and query functions efficiently using a binary search tree. Make sure to handle cases where the events are added or removed in a non-chronological order.
approach: 1. Define a class for Event with properties for start and end times. 2. Implement a binary search tree where each node contains an event and pointers to left and right subtrees. 3. For the add operation, traverse the tree to find the correct location while ensuring no overlapping events are added. 4. For the query operation, traverse the tree to find all events that are active at the specified time, checking each node's start and end times. 5. Ensure that you handle edge cases such as adding/removing events in the wrong order.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly when designing the tree structure. Discuss the trade-offs between different tree structures (like AVL vs. Red-Black trees) for balancing efficiency. Be prepared to discuss edge cases and how your implementation handles them. Consider mentioning time complexity for each operation as you develop your solution.
time_complexity: O(N) for adding and querying in the worst case (unbalanced tree), O(log N) for average case in a balanced tree.
space_complexity: O(N) for storing the events in the tree.

title: String Palindrome Transformation with Limited Operations
description: You are given a string containing only lowercase letters. Your task is to transform this string into a palindrome using the minimum number of character replacements, where each replacement can only change a character at an even index to match a character at an odd index or vice versa. Return the minimum number of replacements required. If it is impossible to make the string a palindrome under these conditions, return -1.
approach: 1. Initialize a counter for replacements. 2. Use two pointers, one starting from the beginning of the string (left) and one from the end (right). 3. Check the characters at these pointers: if they are equal, move both pointers inward. 4. If they are not equal, check if one is at an even index and the other at an odd index. If they are, you can replace one to match the other and increment the replacement counter. 5. If one pointer is odd and the other is even but the characters are not equal, or if they are both even or both odd, it's impossible to make the string a palindromeΓÇöreturn -1. 6. Continue until the pointers meet or cross. 7. Return the replacement count.
interview_tips: Clarify the constraints with the interviewer. Discuss your approach before coding. Consider edge cases such as strings of length 1 or 2. Be mindful of string immutability in some languages.

title: The Mysterious Vending Machine Codes
description: You are given a vending machine that dispenses a variety of snacks. Each snack has a unique code represented as a string of letters. However, the machine has a glitch: it only dispenses snacks if you enter a specific sequence of codes that correspond to a hidden pattern. Your task is to determine if a given sequence of entered codes can be rearranged to match the pattern of codes needed for the vending machine to dispense the snacks. If it can, return 'Dispense', otherwise return 'Error'. You can assume that the codes consist of lowercase letters only and may contain duplicates.
approach: To solve this problem, you can use a frequency count to compare the two sets of codes: the entered sequence and the required pattern. Create a frequency map for both strings, then check if both maps are identical. This ensures that all codes in the required pattern can be formed by rearranging the entered codes.
