interview_tips: Start by clarifying the requirements with the interviewer. Discuss what edge cases might exist (e.g., empty strings, codes with different lengths). Walk through your thought process and consider using data structures like hash maps to store frequency counts. Be prepared to explain your reasoning and optimize if necessary.
time_complexity: O(N), where N is the length of the longer string.
space_complexity: O(1), since the frequency map size is bounded by the number of unique characters (lowercase letters).

title: Bitwise Pyramid: Constructing a Unique Path
description: You are given an integer array representing a pyramid of levels, where each level 'i' contains 'i + 1' integers. The integers in the pyramid are filled with bits (0s and 1s). Your task is to find the minimum path from the top to the bottom of the pyramid where the path value is defined as the result of a bitwise OR operation of all the selected integers along the path. Additionally, if you reach the same path value through different paths, count it only once. Return the count of unique path values that can be obtained from top to bottom of the pyramid. For example, consider the pyramid represented as [[1], [1, 0], [0, 1, 1]]. The unique path values will be calculated through any valid route from the top to bottom. You should implement a function to handle this pyramid structure and compute the result.
approach: 1. Start from the top of the pyramid and explore all possible paths to the bottom using a recursive approach or depth-first search (DFS). 2. For each path taken, compute the cumulative bitwise OR of the selected integers. 3. Use a set to store the unique path values encountered. 4. Once all paths are explored, the size of the set will give the number of unique path values.
interview_tips: Clarify the input structure and discuss your approach before jumping into coding. While writing code, emphasize your understanding of bitwise operations and how they work. Be ready to discuss edge cases, such as empty pyramids or pyramids with all zero values.
time_complexity: O(2^N) where N is the number of levels, as you need to explore multiple paths.
space_complexity: O(N), for storing the path values in the recursion stack.

title: Treasure Hunt: Finding Hidden Markers in a 2D Map
description: You are given a 2D grid representing a map where each cell can either be a marker (denoted by 'M') or an empty space (denoted by 'E'). Your task is to design a function that finds the coordinates of all markers in the grid, but with a twist: you can only traverse the grid using a specific set of movement rules. You can move horizontally or vertically, but you cannot move diagonally. Additionally, you may only proceed to the next cell if it is adjacent to a previously visited marker. If you encounter a new marker, you must add its coordinates to the result. The function should return a list of the coordinates of all markers found, in the order they were discovered. If no markers exist, return an empty list. Example input: [['E', 'E', 'M'], ['M', 'E', 'E'], ['E', 'E', 'M']]. Example output: [(0, 2), (1, 0), (2, 2)]
approach: 1. Initialize a list to store the found marker coordinates. 2. Use a queue to perform a breadth-first search (BFS) or depth-first search (DFS) starting from any marker found in the grid. 3. Track visited cells to avoid cycles. 4. For each marker found, check its adjacent cells in the grid. 5. If an adjacent marker is found and hasn't been visited, add it to the queue and mark it as visited. 6. Continue the process until all reachable markers are discovered. 7. Return the list of coordinates.
interview_tips: Make sure to explain your approach clearly before coding. Discuss edge cases like completely empty grids or grids with no markers. Think aloud while coding and consider discussing optimizations after your initial implementation.
time_complexity: O(N * M), where N is the number of rows and M is the number of columns in the grid.
space_complexity: O(N * M) for the visited list and queue.

title: Mystery Text Reversal
description: You are given a string that contains a mixture of alphanumeric characters, spaces, and punctuation. Your task is to reverse only the alphabetical characters in the string while keeping all other characters (including spaces and punctuation) in their original positions. For example, given the input 'a!b.c?d', the output should be 'd!c.b?a'. Write a function that performs this operation and returns the new string.
approach: 1. Iterate through the string and extract all alphabetical characters into a list while storing their original positions. 2. Reverse the list of alphabetical characters. 3. Create a new result string and iterate through the original string again, replacing the alphabetical positions with characters from the reversed list. 4. Return the newly constructed string.
interview_tips: Clarify the requirements with your interviewer. Ask if there are any specific constraints on input size or character sets. Discuss the approach briefly before diving into coding to ensure youΓÇÖre aligned with the interviewerΓÇÖs expectations.

title: Recursive Treasure Hunt on a Map
description: You are designing a treasure hunting game where players are navigating through a grid-based map. Each cell can either be empty or contain a treasure represented by a positive integer indicating its worth. The player can move up, down, left, or right but cannot move diagonally. The goal is to find the maximum treasure that can be collected starting from the top-left corner of the grid and moving to the bottom-right corner. If a player encounters a cell with zero or a negative value at any point, they must stop and cannot collect any more treasure after that. Implement a recursive function to calculate the maximum treasure that can be collected. You should return the maximum amount of treasure that can be collected if the player follows the allowed paths.
approach: 1. Define a recursive function that takes the current position in the grid as parameters. 2. At each cell, check if it's valid to move to the next cell (i.e., within bounds and not a stopping cell). 3. If valid, recursively call the function for each of the four possible directions (up, down, left, and right) to collect treasures from neighboring cells. 4. Accumulate the treasure values from valid paths and keep track of the maximum treasure collected. 5. Return the maximum treasure found from the starting point to the target point. 6. Ensure to handle cases where the player encounters a cell with zero or negative value, in which case halt that path.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly while implementing the recursive function. Mention edge cases such as boundaries of the grid and stopping conditions explicitly. Use a backtracking approach if necessary to avoid revisiting cells, and ensure to discuss possible optimizations, such as memoization, if time allows.
time_complexity: O(4^(N*M)) where N is the number of rows and M is the number of columns.
space_complexity: O(N + M) for the call stack in the worst case.

title: Tree Construction from Unique Paths
description: Imagine you have a unique set of paths in a forest represented by an array of strings, where each string contains names of nodes in the order they are traversed. Your task is to reconstruct a binary tree such that each unique path represents a unique traversal from the root to a leaf. Each node in the tree has at most two children, and paths are formed by traversing down the left and right children. If the paths are: ['A->B->C', 'A->B->D', 'A->E->F'], reconstruct the binary tree and return the root node of the tree. The tree structure should maintain the order of traversal as indicated by the paths.
approach: 1. Parse each path string to extract the nodes. 2. Initialize a root node. 3. For each path, start from the root and iteratively create child nodes as necessary, ensuring that existing nodes are not duplicated. 4. Link nodes according to the left and right child convention. 5. Return the root node once all paths have been processed.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly while parsing the input and constructing the tree. Pay extra attention to edge cases, such as duplicate nodes and empty paths. Be prepared to discuss the structure of the tree and how it relates to the given paths.
time_complexity: O(P * N), where P is the number of paths and N is the average number of nodes in a path.
space_complexity: O(N), where N is the number of unique nodes in the tree.

title: Sum of Digits
description: Write a recursive function that takes a non-negative integer as input and returns the sum of its digits. For example, if the input is 123, the output should be 6 (1 + 2 + 3). The function should handle large integers and should not use any loops.
approach: 1. Check if the input number is 0. If it is, return 0. 2. Otherwise, separate the last digit from the number using modulus operation (num % 10). 3. Call the function recursively with the number divided by 10 (num // 10). 4. Add the last digit to the result of the recursive call and return the sum.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly while writing the recursive function. Make sure to handle edge cases, such as when the input is 0. Discuss the base case and how the recursion will eventually terminate.

title: Minimum Cost Path in a Grid
description: You are given a 2D grid of integers where each cell represents the cost to enter that cell. You can only move down or right from the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner. Your task is to find the minimum cost to reach the bottom-right corner from the top-left corner. Implement a function that takes the grid as input and returns the minimum cost.
approach: 1. Create a 2D array 'dp' of the same size as the input grid to store the minimum cost to reach each cell. 2. Initialize the top-left cell of 'dp' with the cost of the top-left cell of the grid. 3. Fill the first row and first column of 'dp' by accumulating the costs from the grid. 4. For each remaining cell in 'dp', calculate the minimum cost to reach that cell by taking the minimum of the cell above it and the cell to the left, and adding the cost of the current cell from the grid. 5. The value in the bottom-right cell of 'dp' will be the minimum cost to reach the bottom-right corner.
interview_tips: Make sure to explain your thought process clearly. Discuss the dynamic programming approach and how you are storing intermediate results. Be prepared to analyze the time and space complexity of your solution.

title: Find the Shortest Path in a Graph
description: Given a directed graph represented as an adjacency list, write a function to find the shortest path from a given source node to a target node. If no path exists, return -1. The graph may contain cycles, and you can assume that the nodes are labeled with unique integers starting from 0.
approach: 1. Use Breadth-First Search (BFS) to explore the graph starting from the source node. 2. Maintain a queue to keep track of nodes to visit and a set to track visited nodes. 3. For each node, check its neighbors and enqueue them if they haven't been visited yet. 4. Keep track of the distance from the source node to each visited node. 5. If the target node is reached, return the distance; if the queue is exhausted without finding the target, return -1.
interview_tips: Clarify the input format and constraints before starting. Discuss your thought process and consider edge cases, such as disconnected graphs or graphs with cycles. Be prepared to explain your choice of BFS over DFS for finding the shortest path.

title: Find the Top N Customers by Total Spend
description: You are given a table named 'orders' with the following columns: 'customer_id', 'order_id', and 'total_amount'. Write a SQL query to find the top N customers who have spent the most money in total. The output should include 'customer_id' and the total spend for each customer, ordered by total spend in descending order. If there are ties, include all customers with the same total spend. The value of N will be provided as a parameter.
approach: 1. Use the SUM function to calculate the total spend for each customer by grouping the results by 'customer_id'. 2. Use the ORDER BY clause to sort the results by total spend in descending order. 3. Use the LIMIT clause to restrict the output to the top N customers. 4. Handle ties by using a subquery or a CTE to ensure all customers with the same total spend are included.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify the value of N before starting your query. Discuss your thought process as you write the query, and consider edge cases such as ties in total spend. Be prepared to explain your SQL syntax and any functions you use.

title: Find the Second Highest Salary
description: Write a SQL query to find the second highest salary from the Employee table. If there is no second highest salary, the query should return null. The Employee table has the following structure: Employee(id INT, salary INT).
approach: 1. Use the DISTINCT keyword to eliminate duplicate salaries. 2. Sort the salaries in descending order. 3. Use the LIMIT clause to skip the highest salary and fetch the next one. 4. Handle cases where there might not be a second highest salary by using a subquery or conditional logic.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify the structure of the Employee table and the expected output. Discuss your thought process and consider edge cases, such as when all salaries are the same or when there are fewer than two unique salaries.

title: Find the Shortest Path in a Weighted Graph
description: Given a weighted, directed graph represented as an adjacency list, write a function to find the shortest path from a given source node to a target node. The graph may contain positive weights only. If there is no path from the source to the target, return -1. You can assume that the graph is represented as a dictionary where keys are node identifiers and values are lists of tuples representing connected nodes and their respective weights.
approach: 1. Initialize a priority queue to store nodes along with their current shortest distance from the source. 2. Use a dictionary to track the shortest distance to each node, initializing the source node's distance to 0 and all others to infinity. 3. While the priority queue is not empty, extract the node with the smallest distance. 4. For each neighbor of the current node, calculate the potential new distance. If this distance is smaller than the previously recorded distance, update it and add the neighbor to the priority queue. 5. Repeat until the target node is reached or the queue is empty. 6. If the target node's distance remains infinity, return -1; otherwise, return the distance.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify the input format and constraints before starting. Discuss your thought process and consider edge cases, such as disconnected graphs or nodes with no outgoing edges. Be prepared to explain your choice of algorithm and its efficiency.

title: Sort Array by Parity
description: Given an array of integers, write a function that sorts the array such that all even numbers come before all odd numbers. The relative order of the even and odd numbers should be preserved. For example, given the input array [3, 1, 2, 4], the output should be [2, 4, 3, 1].
approach: 1. Initialize two pointers, one for even numbers and one for odd numbers. 2. Traverse the array and check each number. 3. If the number is even, place it at the front of the array using the even pointer and increment it. 4. If the number is odd, keep it in its place. 5. After processing all numbers, fill in the odd numbers after the even numbers using the odd pointer.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify the requirements, especially regarding the preservation of the relative order of even and odd numbers. Discuss your thought process and consider edge cases, such as an empty array or an array with all even or all odd numbers.

title: Find the Top N Employees by Salary
description: Given a table 'Employees' with columns 'id', 'name', and 'salary', write a SQL query to find the top N employees with the highest salaries. If there are ties, include all employees with the same salary. The value of N will be provided as an input parameter.
approach: 1. Use the 'ORDER BY' clause to sort the employees by salary in descending order. 2. Use the 'LIMIT' clause to restrict the number of results to N. 3. To handle ties, use a subquery or a common table expression (CTE) to first rank the salaries and then filter based on the rank.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify how to handle ties in salaries. Discuss your thought process while writing the query and consider edge cases, such as when there are fewer than N employees.

title: Count Distinct Substrings
description: Given a string, write a function to count the number of distinct substrings in it. A substring is defined as a contiguous sequence of characters within the string. For example, for the string 'abc', the distinct substrings are 'a', 'b', 'c', 'ab', 'bc', and 'abc', totaling 6 distinct substrings.
approach: 1. Initialize a set to store distinct substrings. 2. Use two nested loops to generate all possible substrings of the input string. 3. For each substring, add it to the set. 4. The size of the set at the end will give the count of distinct substrings.
interview_tips: Discuss your thought process clearly and consider edge cases, such as empty strings or strings with repeated characters. Optimize your solution if time permits.

title: Find the Shortest Path in an Unweighted Graph
description: Given an unweighted graph represented as an adjacency list, write a function to find the shortest path from a starting node to a target node. If no path exists, return an empty list. The graph is represented as a dictionary where keys are node identifiers and values are lists of adjacent nodes.
approach: 1. Use Breadth-First Search (BFS) to explore the graph level by level. 2. Initialize a queue with the starting node and a set to keep track of visited nodes. 3. Maintain a dictionary to store the parent of each node to reconstruct the path later. 4. While the queue is not empty, dequeue a node and check if it is the target. If it is, backtrack using the parent dictionary to construct the path. 5. If the target is not found, return an empty list.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly as you implement the BFS algorithm. Discuss edge cases, such as when the graph is empty or when the start and target nodes are the same. Be prepared to analyze the time and space complexity of your solution.

title: Generate All Subsets of a Set
description: Given a set of distinct integers, write a recursive function to generate all possible subsets (the power set). The solution should not include duplicate subsets. For example, for the input set [1, 2, 3], the output should be [[], [1], [2], [3], [1, 2], [1, 3], [2, 3], [1, 2, 3]].
approach: 1. Start with an empty subset and add elements recursively. 2. For each element, decide whether to include it in the current subset or not. 3. Recursively call the function for the next element. 4. Once all elements are processed, add the current subset to the list of subsets. 5. Backtrack to explore other possibilities.
interview_tips: Explain your thought process clearly. Discuss the base case and how you will handle the recursive calls. Make sure to consider edge cases, such as an empty input set. Test your solution with different inputs to ensure correctness.

title: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
description: Given a sorted array of integers and a target value, find the starting and ending position of the target value in the array. If the target is not found in the array, return [-1, -1]. You must write an algorithm with O(log N) time complexity.
approach: 1. Use binary search to find the leftmost index of the target. 2. Use binary search again to find the rightmost index of the target. 3. Return the indices as an array. If the target is not found, return [-1, -1].
interview_tips: Make sure to explain your thought process clearly. Discuss the binary search algorithm and how it can be modified to find both the first and last positions. Be prepared to handle edge cases, such as an empty array or a target that does not exist in the array.

title: Count Vowels in a String
description: Write a function that takes a string as input and returns the count of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) present in the string. The function should be case-insensitive, meaning it should count both uppercase and lowercase vowels. For example, given the input 'Hello World', the output should be 3.
approach: 1. Initialize a counter to zero. 2. Convert the input string to lowercase to handle case insensitivity. 3. Iterate through each character in the string. 4. Check if the character is a vowel by comparing it against a set of vowels. 5. If it is a vowel, increment the counter. 6. After the loop, return the counter as the result.
interview_tips: Make sure to clarify whether the function should handle empty strings or special characters. Discuss your thought process while coding and consider edge cases.

title: Count Set Bits in an Integer
description: Given an integer n, write a function to count the number of set bits (1s) in its binary representation. For example, for n = 5, the binary representation is '101', which has 2 set bits. Your function should return the count of set bits for any non-negative integer input.

title: Find the First Occurrence of a Target in a Sorted Array
description: Given a sorted array of integers and a target integer, write a function to find the index of the first occurrence of the target in the array. If the target is not found, return -1. The function should have a time complexity of O(log n).

title: Sum of Digits in a Number
description: Write a recursive function that takes a non-negative integer as input and returns the sum of its digits. For example, if the input is 123, the output should be 1 + 2 + 3 = 6. The function should handle large integers and should not use any loops.

title: Find the Missing Number in an Array
description: You are given an array containing n distinct numbers taken from 0, 1, 2, ..., n. Your task is to find the one number that is missing from the array. For example, if the input array is [3, 0, 1], the missing number is 2. You must implement a solution that runs in O(n) time complexity and uses O(1) space complexity.

title: Count Leaf Nodes in a Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree, write a function to count the number of leaf nodes in the tree. A leaf node is a node that does not have any children. Implement the function in a way that it traverses the tree and counts the leaf nodes efficiently. You can assume that the tree is non-empty and consists of unique values.

title: Find Top N Employees by Salary
description: Given a table named 'employees' with the following columns: 'id' (integer), 'name' (string), and 'salary' (decimal), write a SQL query to find the top N employees with the highest salaries. The value of N will be provided as a parameter. The result should include the 'id', 'name', and 'salary' of these employees, ordered by salary in descending order. If there are ties in salary, include all tied employees.

title: Count Distinct Elements in an Array
description: Given an array of integers, write a function that returns the count of distinct elements in the array using a HashMap. The function should handle both positive and negative integers and should be efficient in terms of time complexity. For example, given the array [1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5], the function should return 5 since there are five distinct elements: 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5.

title: Sort an Array of Strings by Length
description: Given an array of strings, write a function to sort the array in ascending order based on the length of each string. If two strings have the same length, they should maintain their original order (i.e., stable sorting). For example, given the input ['apple', 'banana', 'fig', 'kiwi', 'grape'], the output should be ['fig', 'kiwi', 'apple', 'grape', 'banana'].

title: Rotate Array
description: Given an array, rotate the array to the right by k steps, where k is non-negative.

title: Valid Anagram
description: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s and false otherwise. An Anagram is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase.

title: Edit Distance
description: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2. You can perform the following operations: insert a character, delete a character, or replace a character.

title: Maximum Subarray Sum
description: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one number) which has the largest sum and return its sum.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
description: Find the kth largest element in an unsorted array. Note that it is the kth largest element in the sorted order, not the kth distinct element. You must solve it in O(n) time complexity.

title: Topological Sort
description: Given a directed acyclic graph (DAG), perform a topological sort and return the ordering of the vertices. You can use either Kahn's algorithm or depth-first search (DFS) for this.

title: Dijkstra's Algorithm
description: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm to find the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph. The graph is represented as an adjacency list.

title: Binary Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: Given a rotated sorted array, find the index of a target value. If the target is not found, return -1. You must implement a solution with O(log n) time complexity.

title: Reverse a String
description: Write a function that reverses a string.
approach: Use two pointers or built-in functions.
concept_explanation: String manipulation is a core skill.
interview_tips: Focus on in-place reversal if possible.

title: LRU Cache
description: Design a data structure that follows the constraints of a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache.

title: Merge Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals, and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.
